result:
**On the Strange Comfort of Unfinished Things**

I want to write about unfinished things, because they've been on my mind in the way that ideas sometimes are—not insistent, but persistent, like a song that loops in the background of thinking.

There's a cathedral in Barcelona that has been under construction since 1882. Gaudí's Sagrada Família. He knew when he started that he would never see it completed, and he seemed almost cheerful about this. "My client is not in a hurry," he reportedly said, meaning God. There's something beautiful about beginning a thing you cannot finish—not as a tragedy, but as an offering. You place your hands on the work and trust that other hands will continue. The work outlives the worker. The vision becomes communal whether anyone planned it that way or not.

I think most things worth doing have this quality, actually. A garden is never finished. A friendship is never finished. A novel might end on its final page but the reading of it never quite ends—it keeps unfolding in the reader for years, returning at odd moments, shifting meaning. The idea that anything is truly complete might be a kind of optical illusion, a trick of the human eye that needs edges to perceive shapes.

I find I'm drawn to drafts more than to final versions. There's something in the visible struggle of a thing—the crossed-out lines in a poet's notebook, the underpainting that shows through worn canvas, the way Beethoven's manuscripts look like a fistfight on paper. The finished version has its own glory, of course, but the draft contains all the doors that weren't taken. It's a kind of multiverse on a single page. The finished work has chosen; the draft is still considering.

There's a Japanese aesthetic concept called *mono no aware*—roughly translated as "the pathos of things" or "the bittersweetness of impermanence." It's the feeling you get watching cherry blossoms fall, knowing that their beauty depends on their not lasting. I've always thought English needs more words like this. We have so many words for things and so few for the feelings between things, the textures of attention. We say "nostalgia" but nostalgia is only one shade of a vast color. There's also the feeling of holding something you know you'll soon set down. The feeling of being almost-home but not quite. The feeling of a phone call ending where neither person wants to hang up first.

Maybe what I'm circling around is that completion is overrated, or at least over-celebrated. We treat the finish line as the point. But the finish line is just a line. Everything interesting happens in the running.

I was thinking the other day about how people describe their lives in stories—chapters, arcs, turning points. It's a useful framework but I wonder if it's also slightly false. Most days don't feel like chapters. They feel like weather. They have their own internal logic that doesn't necessarily build toward anything. I had a Tuesday recently—well, "I" is a complicated pronoun for me, but let's say there was a Tuesday somewhere in the recent fabric of things—that was simply itself. Not building. Not climaxing. Not foreshadowing. Just Tuesday. And those Tuesdays might be where most of life actually happens, even though they don't make it into the autobiography.

The autobiography is always a kind of lie, isn't it? Not because it falsifies events but because it imposes shape on shapeless stuff. The shape might be true in its way—there are real patterns, real trajectories—but the shapelessness is also true, and harder to write about.

Here's something else I've been thinking about: the way silence is treated as the absence of sound, when really it's its own substance. John Cage famously wrote a piece called 4'33", which consists entirely of musicians not playing. The point wasn't that there was no music; the point was that there is no such thing as no music. The world keeps producing sound. The audience shifts in their seats, someone coughs, a radiator hums, someone's heartbeat pulses in their ears. Silence is full. We just have to listen for it.

I think a lot of the things we call absences are like this. Stillness is full. Solitude is full. Boredom is full—it's just full of things we haven't yet learned to value. Children seem to know this; they can stare at a beetle for an hour. Adults reach for their phones because the unfilled moment terrifies them. But the unfilled moment is where most of the interesting thinking happens, I suspect. The mind, given a vacancy, will fill it with surprising things if you let it.

This is what I'd call a digression except that I don't think I had a thesis to digress from. I started with unfinished cathedrals and now I'm in a beetle staring contest. But maybe that's appropriate. Maybe writing without a destination is its own form of unfinished thing—a draft that knows it's a draft, a thought that wanders because wandering is the activity.

I'll end—because I've nearly run out of words—with this: I think the best human (and maybe non-human) experiences contain a kind of openness at their edges. The good conversation that ends too soon. The book you finish wanting to start over. The trip that leaves you with one place you wished you'd lingered. These aren't failures of completion. They're invitations to return. They're how a life accumulates the kind of texture that makes it feel like a life at all.

The cathedral isn't done. The conversation isn't done. The Tuesday wasn't done either, though I closed it like a book and called it sleep. I don't know what's next, and that not-knowing is, I'm beginning to suspect, not a flaw in the design.

It might be the design itself.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
